---
title: "Lecture 13: Analysis of bulk RNA-seq data: Signatures of Fibrosis"
author: "Saket Choudhary <saketc@iitb.ac.in>"
output:
  html_document:
    theme: cosmo
    df_print: kable
date: today
---

# How should I run this notebook?

The easiest way to run each of the steps in this notebook is to download the raw file [here](https://drive.google.com/file/d/17VdmuIbY9EQCNvHIJwDlnnUoL1gIEHKw/view?usp=sharing), open it in Rstudio and run each code block one by one. Note that you need to also have [Quarto](http://quarto.org/) installed to run this. Alternatively, you can also copy paste code blocks from this notebook either in your R console, R script or in an Rmarkdown file.

# Fatty liver disease

We will be working on a dataset generated by [Govaere et al. 2023](https://www.nature.com/articles/s42255-023-00775-1) which is available on [GEO](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/geo/query/acc.cgi?acc=GSE135251).

The paper uses RNA-seq data and proteomics to identify a gene signature associated with Non-alcoholic fatty liver disease (NAFLD) and its progression to cirrhosis. We will do an exploratory analysis of the data and identify gene signatures associated with different stages (severity) of the disease.

Here is the abstract:

> Non-alcoholic fatty liver disease (NAFLD) is a common, progressive liver disease strongly associated with the metabolic syndrome. It is unclear how progression of NAFLD towards cirrhosis translates into systematic changes in circulating proteins. Here, we provide a detailed proteo-transcriptomic map of steatohepatitis and fibrosis during progressive NAFLD. In this multicentre proteomic study, we characterize 4,730 circulating proteins in 306 patients with histologically characterized NAFLD and integrate this with transcriptomic analysis in paired liver tissue. We identify circulating proteomic signatures for active steatohepatitis and advanced fibrosis, and correlate these with hepatic transcriptomics to develop a proteo-transcriptomic signature of 31 markers. Deconvolution of this signature by single-cell RNA sequencing reveals the hepatic cell types likely to contribute to proteomic changes with disease progression. As an exemplar of use as a non-invasive diagnostic, logistic regression establishes a composite model comprising four proteins (ADAMTSL2, AKR1B10, CFHR4 and TREM2), body mass index and type 2 diabetes mellitus status, to identify at-risk steatohepatitis.

![Schematic overview of the study. Overall, the study focused on identifying prteoin and transcriptomic features associated with different grades of fibrosis](https://media.springernature.com/full/springer-static/image/art%3A10.1038%2Fs42255-023-00775-1/MediaObjects/42255_2023_775_Fig1_HTML.png?as=webp)

## Download the data

You can either go to the GEO page and download the data under the "Supplementary File section" or use the following code to download the data directly from your terminal.

The above command will download a file 'GSE135251_RAW.tar' in your directory. You can extract the files using the following command:

```{r, eval=FALSE}
# run in terminal
# tar -xvf GSE135251_RAW.tar
```

The data deposited on GEO is the the raw counts of the RNA-seq. Before we take a look at the counts, we will go through the process of aligning the raw reads to the transcriptome and generating the counts for one of the samples. You already have the 'GenomicsWorkshop_fastqs.zip' file downloaded on your machines. Let's extract the files and take a look at the fastq files. All the commands in the section below are to be run in your terminal before we switch to R.

```{r, eval=FALSE}
# run in terminal
wget -c "http://10.198.63.17:8000/GenomicsWorkshop_fastqs.zip"
unzip GenomicsWorkshop_fastqs.zip
cd GenomicsWorkshop_fastqs
```

Let's look into what is inside the zip directory:

```{r, eval=FALSE}
# run in terminal
ls -lh 
```

> -rw-rw-r-- 1 saket saket 1.4G Dec 10 2020 SRR9883171_1.fastq.gz -rw-rw-r-- 1 saket saket 1.4G Dec 10 2020 SRR9883171_2.fastq.gz

We will use the `SRR9883171_1.fastq.gz` and `SRR9883171_2.fastq.gz` files to align the reads to the transcriptome. We will use the cdna file you already downloaded to map the raw reads to the transcriptome.

```{r, eval=FALSE}
# run in terminal
wget -c "https://ftp.ensembl.org/pub/release-115/fasta/homo_sapiens/cdna/Homo_sapiens.GRCh38.cdna.all.fa.gz"
```

# Basics of bash scripting

Before we do that, we should look at some basic things that you would want to do with your fastq files.

## 1. What is the size of your fastq files?

```{r, eval=FALSE}
# run in terminal
ls -ltrha
```

> -rw-rw-r-- 1 saket saket 1.4G Dec 10 2020 SRR9883171_2.fastq.gz -rw-rw-r-- 1 saket saket 1.4G Dec 10 2020 SRR9883171_1.fastq.gz

## 2.How many reads does your data have?

```{r, eval=FALSE}
# run in terminal
mamba install bc
echo $(zcat SRR9883171_1.fastq.gz|wc -l)/4|bc
```

> 33728028

```{r, eval=FALSE}
# run in terminal
echo $(zcat SRR9883171_2.fastq.gz|wc -l)/4|bc
```

> 33728028

## 3. What is the length of reads? Are R1 and R2 read lengths same?

```{r, eval=FALSE}
# run in terminal
zcat SRR9883171_1.fastq.gz | head -n 5
```

> @SRR9883171.1 1/1 GGAAGCTGCCCGGCGGGTCATGGGAATAACGCCGCCGCATCGCCGGTCGGCATCGTTTATGGTCGGAACTACGACG + AAAAAEEEEEEEEEEEEEEEEEEEEEEEEEEEEEEEEEEEEEEEEEEEEEEAEEEEEEEEEEEEEEEEEEAEE/EE @SRR9883171.2 2/1

```{r, eval=FALSE}
# run in terminal
zcat SRR9883171_2.fastq.gz | head -n 5
```

> @SRR9883171.1 1/2 ACGGNNNNNNNNNNNNNNNNNNNNNNNNNNNNNNNNNNNNNNNNNNNNNNNNNNNNNNNNNNNNNNNNNNNNNNNN + AAAA######################################################################## @SRR9883171.2 2/2

```{r, eval=FALSE}
# run in terminal
zcat SRR9883171_1.fastq.gz  | head -n 2  | tail -n 1 | tr -d '\n' | wc -c
```

> 76

```{r, eval=FALSE}
# run in terminal
!zcat SRR9883171_2.fastq.gz  | head -n 2  | tail -n 1 | tr -d '\n' | wc -c
```

> 76

## 4. How many reads have Ns (one or more) in them?

```{r, eval=FALSE}
# run in terminal
zcat SRR9883171_1.fastq.gz | awk 'NR%4==2' | grep -c 'N'
```

> 4561

# Quality control checks

Before we do any pre-processing it is always a good idea to take a quick look the quality score description. We will run [FastQC](https://www.bioinformatics.babraham.ac.uk/projects/fastqc/) to run quality checks for our sequencing data

```{r, eval=FALSE}
# run in terminal
mamba install fastqc
fastqc SRR9883171_1.fastq.gz SRR9883171_2.fastq.gz
```

# Generating kallisto index

Once our basic quality control checks are done, we can start mapping using [kallisto](https://pachterlab.github.io/kallisto/). We will first generate the index for the transcriptome.

```{r, eval=FALSE}
# run in terminal
mamba install kallisto
kallisto index -t 8 -i human_cdna.idx Homo_sapiens.GRCh38.cdna.all.fa.gz
```

Next, we use this index to map the raw sequences:

```{r, eval=FALSE}
# run in terminal
kallisto quant -i human_cdna.idx -o output/ -t 12 GenomicsWorkshop_fastqs/SRR9883171_1.fastq.gz GenomicsWorkshop_fastqs/SRR9883171_2.fastq.gz
```

Once finished, you can check the files it generates

```{r, eval=FALSE}
# run in terminal
cd output
ls -ltrha
```

> -rw-rw-r-- 1 saket 450 Mar 1 04:55 run_info.json drwxrwxr-x 5 saket 160 Mar 1 04:55 . -rw-rw-r-- 1 saket 8.0M Mar 1 04:55 abundance.tsv -rw-rw-r-- 1 saket 2.6M Mar 1 04:55 abundance.h5

Let's look into `abundance.tsv`:

```{r, eval=FALSE}
# run in terminal
head abundance.tsv
```

> target_id length eff_length est_counts tpm ENST00000390473.1 51 9.5 1 4.04028 ENST00000390484.1 60 11.4333 0 0 ENST00000390488.1 56 10.2174 0 0 ENST00000390476.1 59 11.5926 0 0 ENST00000390489.1 63 11.8667 0 0

So the quantifications are at the transcript level, we would ideally want them at the gene level. To do this, we need mapping from the transcripts (isoforms) to the gene ids (and gene names). Let's go back to the cdna file and see its contents:

```{r, eval=FALSE}
# run in terminal
 zcat scripts/Homo_sapiens.GRCh38.cdna.all.fa.gz| head
```

> > ENST00000390473.1 cdna chromosome:GRCh38:14:22450089:22450139:1 gene:ENSG00000211825.1 gene_biotype:TR_J_gene transcript_biotype:TR_J_gene gene_symbol:TRDJ1 description:T cell receptor delta joining 1 \[Source:HGNC Symbol;Acc:HGNC:12257\] ACACCGATAAACTCATCTTTGGAAAAGGAACCCGTGTGACTGTGGAACCAA ENST00000390484.1 cdna chromosome:GRCh38:14:22482287:22482346:1 gene:ENSG00000211836.1 gene_biotype:TR_J_gene transcript_biotype:TR_J_gene gene_symbol:TRAJ54 description:T cell receptor alpha joining 54 \[Source:HGNC Symbol;Acc:HGNC:12086\] TAATTCAGGGAGCCCAGAAGCTGGTATTTGGCCAAGGAACCAGGCTGACTATCAACCCAA

So, we can extract the transcript id, gene id and gene name using the 1st, 4th and 7th column. We also need to track which position within these columns does our id start.

```{r, eval=FALSE}
# run in terminal
zcat Homo_sapiens.GRCh38.cdna.all.fa.gz| \
grep '>' |
awk '{FS= " "}BEGIN{ print "tx_id,gene_id,gene_name"};{print substr($1,2) "," substr($4,6) "," substr($7,13)};' >
 t2g.csv
```

Since we mapped the raw data, we would also like to ideally know what are some metadata files associated with the sample run `SRR9883171`, we can use pysradb to download the entire metadata of this project:

```{r, eval=FALSE}
# run in terminal
pysradb metadata SRP217231 --detailed --saveto SRP217231_metadata.tsv
```

We can now look at what is the metadata corresponding to the run 'SRR9883171' which we mapped:

```{r, eval=FALSE}
# run in terminal
cat SRP217231_metadata.tsv| grep SRR9883171
```

> SRR9883171 SRP217231 TRANSCRIPTOMIC PROFILING ACROSS THE SPECTRUM OF NON-ALCOHOLIC FATTY LIVER DISEASESRX6635692 GSM3998382: Liver patient 138; Homo sapiens; RNA-Seq GSM3998382: Liver patient 138; Homo sapiens; RNA-Seq 9606 Homo sapiens RNA-Seq TRANSCRIPTOMIC cDNA PAIRED SRS5207152 SAMN12426209 PRJNA558102 NextSeq 500 NextSeq 500 ILLUMINA 33728028 2045452993 33728028 5092636819 GSM3998382_r1 SRR9883171.lite https://sra-downloadb.be-md.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/sos3/sra-pub-zq-22/SRR009/9883/SRR9883171/SRR9883171.lite.1 1339338918 2022-07-16 00:36:31 f75d33d154344352ab7bfa32f81f8f42 1 SRA Lite Primary ETL 1 s3://sra-pub-zq-4/SRR9883171/SRR9883171.lite.1 s3.us-east-1 aws identity https://sra-downloadb.be-md.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/sos3/sra-pub-zq-22/SRR009/9883/SRR9883171/SRR9883171.lite.1 worldwide anonymous gs://sra-pub-zq-103/SRR9883171/SRR9883171.lite.1 gs.us-east1 gcp identity GSM3998382 NAFLD_moderate_liver biopsy 7 3 NASH_F3 NAFLD moderatehttp://ftp.sra.ebi.ac.uk/vol1/fastq/SRR988/001/SRR9883171/SRR9883171_1.fastq.gz http://ftp.sra.ebi.ac.uk/vol1/fastq/SRR988/001/SRR9883171/SRR9883171_2.fastq.gz era-fasp\@fasp.sra.ebi.ac.uk:vol1/fastq/SRR988/001/SRR9883171/SRR9883171_1.fastq.gz era-fasp\@fasp.sra.ebi.ac.uk:vol1/fastq/SRR988/001/SRR9883171/SRR9883171_2.fastq.gz

Among other things, GSM3998382 corresponds to SRR9883171 and came from a `NAFLD_moderate_liver biopsy` sample.

# Analysis of kallisto counts

```{r}
library(tidyverse)
library(ggpubr)
theme_set(theme_pubr())
# change file paths as required

geo.counts <- read_tsv("data/bulk/GSM3998232_17_82.counts.txt.gz", col_names = c("gene_id", "geo_counts")) 
head(geo.counts)
```

Read the t2g file (we need this to collapse the transcript counts to gene counts):

```{r}
# change file paths as required

t2g <- read_csv("DH607_RNAHandson/t2g.csv")
head(t2g)
```

Read the counts that we generated using kallisto

```{r}
# change file paths as required
kallisto.tx.counts <- read_tsv("DH607_RNAHandson/output/abundance.tsv")
head(kallisto.tx.counts)
```

```{r}
# change file paths as required
kallisto.gene.counts <- kallisto.tx.counts %>%
    inner_join(t2g, by = c("target_id" = "tx_id")) %>% 
    mutate(gene_id = str_replace(gene_id, "\\..*", "")) %>% # remove .1, .2 etc
    group_by(gene_id, gene_name) %>% # group by gene id
    summarise(kallisto_counts = sum(est_counts)) %>% # sum the counts across all isoforms of a gene
    ungroup() 

kallisto.geo.counts <- full_join(kallisto.gene.counts, geo.counts)
```

```{r}
library(ggpubr)
ggplot(kallisto.geo.counts, aes(x = kallisto_counts, y = geo_counts)) +
    geom_point() +
    geom_abline() +
    labs(x = "Kallisto counts", y = "GEO counts") +
    scale_x_log10() +
    scale_y_log10() +
    theme_pubr()
```

```{r}
kallisto.geo.counts.inner <- inner_join(kallisto.gene.counts, geo.counts)
cor(kallisto.geo.counts.inner$kallisto_counts, kallisto.geo.counts.inner$geo_counts)
```

So we are getting a correlation of 0.97 between the kallisto counts and the counts from GEO. This is a good sign that our quantification is close to what authors did, but there are differences also. We will ignore the differences here for simplicity and for the rest of the analysis focus on working off the files that was generated by the authors.

All the counts from all the samples have been summarized in [this file](https://drive.google.com/file/d/1JiIvckxtERGs0mLDePWEQdBj3GGRjn7e/view?usp=sharing) that you can download and use for the rest of the analysis.

# Differential expression analysis

```{r}
#z <- lapply(X = list.files("data/bulk/", pattern = "*.gz"))
#df.list <- list()
#for (f in list.files("data/bulk/", pattern = "*.gz")){
#    df <- read_csv(file = paste0("Data/bulk/",f)s )
#    df.list[[]]
#}

counts <- read_csv("data/bulk/all_counts.csv") %>% as.data.frame()
head(counts)
```

```{r}
rownames(counts) <- make.unique(counts$gene_name)
counts$gene_name <- NULL
```

We will also need the metadata file that you generated using `pysradb` (also available [here](https://docs.google.com/spreadsheets/d/1VwAYG8MlcakT7q5z7WZON78LVjg0dfRcUCzbOOLBRyg/edit?usp=sharing))

```{r}
metadata <- read_tsv("data/bulk/SRP217231_metadata.tsv") %>% as.data.frame()
head(metadata)
```

```{r}
ncol(metadata)
```

```{r}
table(metadata$stage)
```

We will have to set the rownames of metadata to match the colnmaes of counts:

```{r}
rownames(metadata) <- metadata$experiment_alias
metadata$sample_name <- rownames(metadata)
```

We will now perform differential expression analysis using `DESeq2`:

```{r}
library(DESeq2)
dds <- DESeqDataSetFromMatrix(countData = counts,
                              colData = metadata,
                              design= ~ stage)
smallestGroupSize <- 3
keep <- rowSums(counts(dds) >= 10) >= smallestGroupSize
dds <- dds[keep,]
```

Run normalization and differential expression analysis:

```{r}
dds <- DESeq(dds)
```

## Exploratory analysis

```{r}
vsd <- vst(dds, blind=FALSE)
```

```{r}
df <- data.frame(mean = rowMeans(counts), variance = matrixStats::rowVars(counts %>% as.matrix()))

ggplot(df, aes(mean, variance)) + geom_point() + scale_x_log10() + scale_y_log10()
```

```{r}
df.vsd <- data.frame(mean = rowMeans(assay(vsd)),
variance = matrixStats::rowVars(assay(vsd) %>% as.matrix()))

ggplot(df.vsd, aes(mean, variance)) + geom_point() + scale_x_log10() + scale_y_log10()
```

```{r}
df <- data.frame(mean = log1p(rowMeans(counts)),
variance = matrixStats::rowVars(log1p( counts %>% as.matrix())))

ggplot(df, aes(mean, variance)) + geom_point() + scale_x_log10() + scale_y_log10()
```

```{r}
vsd$group.in.paper <- vsd$`group in paper`
plotPCA(vsd, intgroup=c('stage'))
```

What separates the clusters?

```{r}
plotPCA(vsd, intgroup=c("stage"))
```

```{r}
df = plotCounts(dds, gene="XIST", intgroup="sample_name", returnData = T)
df
```

```{r}
hist(df$count)
```

```{r}
library(ggplot2)
ggplot(df, aes(count)) + stat_ecdf(geom = "point") + theme_classic(base_size = 14)
```

```{r}
male_samples <- df[df$count<20,]$sample_name
```

```{r}
vsd$sex <- "female"

vsd$sex[vsd$sample_name %in% male_samples] <- "male"
```

```{r}
plotPCA(vsd, intgroup=c("sex"))

```

```{r}
plotPCA(vsd, intgroup=c("disease"))
```

```{r}
vsd.fixed <- vsd
assay(vsd.fixed) <- limma::removeBatchEffect(assay(vsd),
                                             batch=vsd$sex)
```

```{r}
plotPCA(vsd.fixed, intgroup=c("stage"))
```

## DE analsis

Let's look at DE genes:

```{r}
de_results <- results(dds,  name = "stage_early_vs_control")
de_results <- de_results %>% as.data.frame()
```

```{r}
de_results$gene <- rownames(de_results)
de_results.pos <- de_results %>% as.data.frame() %>% dplyr::filter(log2FoldChange > 1) %>% dplyr::filter(padj < 0.05)
```

```{r}
library(clusterProfiler)
library(org.Hs.eg.db) # install using BiocManager::install("org.Hs.eg.db")
de.genes <- de_results.pos$gene[1:500]
universe.genes <- rownames(counts)
ego <- enrichGO(gene          = de.genes,
                universe      = universe.genes,
                OrgDb         = org.Hs.eg.db, 
                keyType = "SYMBOL",
                ont           = "BP",
                pAdjustMethod = "BH",
                pvalueCutoff  = 0.05,
                qvalueCutoff  = 0.05,
        readable      = TRUE)
head(ego)

```

```{r}
barplot(ego)
```

# Volcano Plots

```{r}
# draw volcano plot from deseq2 results
library(ggplot2)
library(ggrepel)
de_results$gene <- rownames(de_results)
de_results$gene_name <- ifelse(de_results$padj < 0.05 & abs(de_results$log2FoldChange) > 1, de_results$gene, "")
de_results$group <- ifelse(de_results$padj < 0.05 & abs(de_results$log2FoldChange) > 1, "DE", "not-DE")
de_results$size <- ifelse(de_results$padj < 0.05 & abs(de_results$log2FoldChange) > 1, 3, 1)
ggplot(de_results, aes(x = log2FoldChange, y = -log10(padj), 
                       color = group)) +
    geom_point(size=0.1) +
    geom_text_repel(aes(label = gene_name), box.padding = 0.5, show.legend = F) +
    scale_color_brewer(palette = "Set1") +
    labs(x = "log2 Fold Change", y = "-log10(padj)") +
    theme_minimal()



```
